Summary: TCF7L2 gene variants contribute to the pathogenesis of type 2 diabetes and predict disease onset in human cohorts. The genetic association has been consistently replicated across diverse populations and a meta-analysis indicates a multiplicative genetic model. These variants are estimated to be involved in nearly one-fifth of T2D cases. Variant rs290487 contributed to insulin resistance and implicated Wnt signaling. In a French cohort, carriers of TCF7L2 gene variants showed increased glycemia, decreased insulin secretion, and a more rapid reduction of pancreatic β-cell function. The effects observed in carriers of TCF7L2 variants in the French cohort (more rapid reduction of β-cell function, increased glycemia, decreased insulin secretion) were related to familial history of type 2 diabetes. The role of TCF7L2 variants in complex regulatory functions remains little explored. Resequencing of the TCF7L2 gene in diverse ethnic groups will allow identification of common and rare genetic variants that contribute to the pathogenesis of type 2 diabetes. Therapies that modulate TCF7L2 expression may offer treatment opportunities for risk-allele carriers.
Reference: Del Bosque-Plata, L., Martínez-Martínez, E., Espinoza-Camacho, M. Á., & Gragnoli, C. (2021). The Role of TCF7L2 in Type 2 Diabetes. Diabetes, 70(6), 1220-1228. https://doi.org/10.2337/db20-0573

Chang, S., Wang, Z., Wu, L., Lu, X., Shangguan, S., Xin, Y., Li, L., & Wang, L. (2017). Association between TCF7L2 polymorphisms and gestational diabetes mellitus: A meta-analysis. J Diabetes Investig, 8(4), 560-570. https://doi.org/10.1111/jdi.12612
Note: No function was revealed by this paper.

Summary: TCF7L2 rs7903146T is a long-established and consistently replicated genetic risk factor for type 2 diabetes in humans. A multiethnic GWAS identified rs7903146T as the strongest detected T2DM susceptibility variant. In regional cohorts the association persisted except in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. In a high‑risk Kuwaiti population rs7903146T was the most consistent T2DM risk variant. Functional studies showed rs7903146T influences impaired insulin secretion. In the sampled Kuwaiti population the rs7903146 TT genotype associated with increased T2DM risk in an autosomal recessive model (OR 3.382, 95% CI 1.99–5.92, p < 0.0001). Allelic and genotypic frequencies of rs7903146 differed between healthy controls and T2DM patients and the T allele frequency in the sampled healthy Kuwaiti population exceeded frequencies recorded in the ALFA/dbGaP database. The variant was also associated with peripheral neuropathy and myocardial infarction. The variant was suggested to have potential predictive clinical application for T2DM and its complications.
Reference: Chaudhary, N., Alawadhi, F., Al-Serri, A., & Al-Temaimi, R. (2024). TCF7L2 and FTO Polymorphisms Are Associated with Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus Risk in Kuwait. Med Princ Pract, 33(2), 157-163. https://doi.org/10.1159/000536229

Summary: A comprehensive review and meta-analysis of published literature on TCF7L2 (and CAPN10) gene polymorphisms in relation to gestational diabetes mellitus was conducted. In gestational diabetes mellitus, TCF7L2 may directly affect pancreatic islet β-cells. This reduces secretion of insulin and glucagon-like peptides and thereby increases endogenous glucose production. Single nucleotide polymorphisms in TCF7L2 have been reported to associate with onset of gestational diabetes during pregnancy, and the study meta-analyzed five loci reported in a large number of articles to determine genetic susceptibility to gestational diabetes. In type 2 diabetes mellitus, TCF7L2 is reported as an important gene associated with disease pathogenesis. In that context, TCF7L2 may interfere with GLP1, reduce GLP1R and GIP-R expression, inhibit β cell function by stimulating insulin secretion in conjunction with GIP, cause abnormal insulin conversion, and activate the Wnt signaling pathway. Most prior studies examined a limited number of loci and yielded inconsistent findings.
Reference: He, J., Zhang, M., Ren, J., & Jiang, X. (2024). Correlation between TCF7L2 and CAPN10 gene polymorphisms and gestational diabetes mellitus in different geographical regions: a meta-analysis. BMC Pregnancy Childbirth, 24(1), 15. https://doi.org/10.1186/s12884-023-06177-1

Summary: TCF7L2 is described as the strongest susceptibility gene for T2DM based on multiple studies worldwide. Meta-analyses found associations between the TCF7L2 variants rs4506565, rs7901695, rs11196205, and rs12255372 and T2DM in Asians and Caucasians, but population-specific results vary. rs7901695 was associated with T2DM in Greek Cypriot and Moyu County Uygur samples and in a Chinese meta-analysis, while several Han Chinese studies did not find a significant association. TCF7L2 is mainly expressed in human islet beta cells and adipose tissue and its variation is linked to decreased insulin secretion, possibly due to reduced pancreatic β‑cell function, although the pathogenesis is not fully elucidated. rs7903146 is intronic and is in perfect linkage disequilibrium (R2=1) with rs7901695, which is an exon CpG‑site. In a multiple logistic regression analysis with T2DM as independent variable, the rs7901695 genotype correlated with genetic susceptibility to T2DM in unadjusted additive and dominant models (p < 0.05) and showed no significant correlation in the unadjusted recessive model (p > 0.05). After adjusting for covariates, the rs7901695 genotype correlated with genetic susceptibility to T2DM in additive, dominant, and recessive models (p < 0.05).
Reference: Su, Y., Shang, B., Hu, X., Zhang, Z., Wang, L., Luo, K., Yao, H., Liu, X., Lu, Y., & Jiang, S. (2025). Association Between ABCG1/TCF7L2 and Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus: An Intervention Trial Based on a Case-Control Study. J Diabetes Res, 2025, 9356676. https://doi.org/10.1155/jdr/9356676

Summary: TCF7L2 is a high mobility group box-containing transcription factor and a critical component of Wnt signaling. The TCF7L2 gene product acts through regulation of proglucagon gene expression in enteroendocrine cells via the Wnt signaling pathway. In human islets, TCF7L2 expression associates positively with insulin gene expression. Large-scale association studies linked common variants in TCF7L2 to susceptibility to type 2 diabetes mellitus, and a meta-analysis was performed because study quality varied and results were inconsistent across populations. The single-nucleotide polymorphism rs7903146 carries allele T as the reported risk allele for T2DM. Grant et al. reported strong association of common variants in TCF7L2 with T2DM in Icelandic subjects and replication occurred in Danish and U.S. cohorts. The T-allele of rs7903146 was reported to increase T2DM risk in French, Japanese, African American, and Brasilia populations. Zheng et al. found no association between rs7903146 and T2DM in the Chinese population.
Reference: Ding, W., Xu, L., Zhang, L., Han, Z., Jiang, Q., Wang, Z., & Jin, S. (2018). Meta-analysis of association between TCF7L2 polymorphism rs7903146 and type 2 diabetes mellitus. BMC Med Genet, 19(1), 38. https://doi.org/10.1186/s12881-018-0553-5
